title: Deep audit: 4 bugs fixed in retirement_mc.py and drawdown_analyzer.py

bugs_found: CRITICAL: retirement_mc.py compute_safe_withdrawal_rate used (1.025/12)^m instead of (1+0.025/12)^m for inflation — formula evaluates to 0.0854^m which goes to zero after month 1, making all withdrawal rates appear safe (SWR computed as 10% instead of ~2.6%)
MATH: drawdown_analyzer.py Sortino ratio used std() (which subtracts mean) instead of sqrt(mean(min(r-rf,0)^2)) for downside deviation, and didn't use excess returns (r-rf) before filtering downside
OFF-BY-ONE: drawdown_analyzer.py rolling max drawdown used prices.iloc[i-window:i] excluding the current day — a crash on the current day would not be captured
REPRODUCIBILITY: retirement_mc.py binary search shared mutable RNG across _ruin_rate calls — each iteration consumed different random numbers, making results path-dependent
observation.gap_identified: Two core analytics modules (retirement MC simulator and drawdown analyzer) contained mathematical errors that produced wrong results. The inflation bug was catastrophic — it made the safe withdrawal rate calculator fundamentally broken, reporting ~10% safe rates instead of ~3%.

bugs_fixed: Fixed inflation formula: (1.025/12)^m -> (1 + 0.025/12)^m. SWR went from 10% to 2.57% for $1M/30yr/moderate/95% target.
Fixed Sortino ratio: changed from std() on zeroed returns to sqrt(mean(min(r-rf,0)^2)). Now properly uses excess returns and RMS instead of standard deviation.
Fixed rolling max drawdown off-by-one: prices.iloc[i-window:i] -> prices.iloc[i-window:i+1] to include current day.
Fixed binary search RNG: each _ruin_rate() call now creates a fresh RNG from the seed, making results deterministic and path-independent.
implementation.feature_built: 7 regression tests covering all 4 bugs (would have caught them pre-fix)

limitations: Only audited 8 service files out of 55 total
technical_analysis.py still has 5 broad excepts (flagged but not fixed — those are intentional graceful degradation)
assessment.self_critique: Should have also audited monte_carlo.py and crash_model.py — the highest-stakes code. The inflation bug was a classic copy-paste error that could have been caught by a simple property test (inflation grows over time). Would add property-based tests in next cycle.

next_steps: Audit monte_carlo.py and crash_model.py for similar math bugs
Add property-based tests (hypothesis) for financial formulas
Narrow broad excepts in technical_analysis.py to specific exceptions
